factors.summary: The company faces significant geopolitical risks, particularly in regions like North Africa, Russia, and the Middle East, which could impact global oil and gas producers. No specific information on geopolitical factors affecting the company.

factors.summary: The company faces significant near-term risks primarily related to the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, including supply chain disruptions, logistics constraints, and labor shortages. Additionally, there are concerns about economic and political factors affecting cash flows, as well as currency effects and reduced sales volumes in various regions.

evidence.text: Specific factors that might cause such a difference include, without limitation, the following: • uncertainties related to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on our business and operations, financial results and financial position, our customers and suppliers, and on the global economy, including its impact on our sales;
evidence.subsection: ACCOUNTING DEVELOPMENTS / Cautionary Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

evidence.text: Cash flows from operations could be adversely affected by economic, political and other risks associated with sales of our products, operational factors, competition, fluctuations in foreign exchange rates and fluctuations in interest rates, among other factors.

Currency effects provided a decrease of approximately $42 million. The decreased sales were primarily driven by original equipment, with decreased sales into Europe, North America, Asia Pacific, the Middle East and Africa, partially offset by increased sales into Latin America.

These conditions have had an adverse effect on the speed at which we can manufacture and ship our products to customers, and have also led to an increase in logistics, transportation and freight costs, requiring that we diversify our supply chain and, in some instances, source materials from new suppliers. Additionally, these conditions have in some cases impacted our ability to deliver products to customers on time, which has in turn led to an increase in backlog at some of our manufacturing sites. These disruptions in our supply chain and their effects have continued and we expect they will continue as the COVID-19 pandemic and ongoing global supply chain and logistics headwinds continue.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant near-term risks from COVID-19 impact, supply chain issues, and economic factors.

factors.summary: The supply chain is facing significant disruptions due to global logistics constraints, increased freight costs, and ongoing challenges from the COVID-19 pandemic. factors.summary: The company's margins are under significant pressure, primarily due to lower gross profit margins and operational challenges. Factors contributing to this include lower margin orders, supply chain disruptions, increased logistics costs, and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, such as underutilized capacity and labor availability issues. While the company is implementing cost-saving measures, these efforts may not be sufficient to offset the current pressures.

evidence.text: These risks and uncertainties may cause actual results to differ materially from what is forecast in such forward-looking statements and are currently, or in the future could be, amplified by the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin for the nine months ended September 30, 2021 of 29.4% decreased from 29.9% for the same period in 2020. The decrease in gross profit margin was primarily attributable to revenue recognized on lower margin original equipment orders, partially offset by a $5.8 million charge of underutilized capacity manufacturing costs expensed related to the COVID-19 pandemic that did not recur and decreased charges and increased savings under our realignment actions as compared to the same period in 2020.
Gross profit margin for the three months ended September 30, 2021 of 29.3% decreased from 30.9% for the same period in 2020. Operating income as a percentage of sales decreased from 9.4% to 6.6%.

We have not seen and do not expect to see an increase in cancellations from our backlog. We therefore expect to continue to deliver on our backlog during the fourth quarter and into 2022, though with a slightly longer cycle time than originally expected.

factors.summary: Demand trends are mixed, with some industries experiencing increases while others see decreases. Overall demand is decelerating, as evidenced by decreasing bookings and sales. Despite modest improvements in customer spending, the pandemic continues to constrain demand and cause delays.

evidence.text: Bookings for the three months ended September 30, 2021 increased by $16.0 million, or 6.7%, as compared with the same period in 2020. The increase in customer bookings was primarily driven by increased orders in the general and water industries, partially offset by decreased bookings in the oil and gas and power generation industries.

Bookings for the three months ended September 30, 2021 increased by $105.5 million, or 13.1%, as compared with the same period in 2020. However, sales decreased by $58.2 million, or 6.3%, as compared with the same period in 2020.

Despite the modest improvement in customer spending, during the third quarter we experienced an increase in customer-driven delays in the witnessing and inspection necessary to take delivery of equipment, which we expect will continue as long as we and our customers continue to experience the supply chain and logistics headwinds described below under the heading 'Supply Chain Impact.'

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Decelerating demand with mixed industry performance and supply chain constraints.

factors.summary: The end market momentum is mixed across different industries. While the general, chemical, and power generation industries are showing growth, the oil and gas industry is slowing down. Additionally, the COVID-19 pandemic has negatively impacted the oil and gas and chemical industries, leading to reduced capital expenditures and slower demand.

evidence.text: The increase in customer bookings was primarily driven by increased orders in the general, chemical and power generation industries, partially offset by decreased orders in the oil and gas industry.

The increase in customer bookings was driven by increased orders in the oil and gas, chemical, power generation, and water industries and were partially offset by decreased bookings in the general industries.

During the first nine months of 2021, the ongoing effects of the COVID-19 pandemic in global markets has continued to adversely impact our customers, particularly in the oil and gas markets. As a result of the pandemic’s effect (among certain other effects) on oil prices during 2020, many of our large customers reduced capital expenditures and budgets last year. To date, while spending for maintenance and repair projects and aftermarket services have returned close to pre-pandemic levels, project-based customer spending has yet to return to pre-pandemic levels despite some modest improvement in the first nine months of 2021.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Growth in some industries but decline in oil and gas and impacts from COVID-19.

The combined effects of the supply chain, logistics and labor availability headwinds discussed above shifted approximately $60 million of expected revenue out of the third quarter and impacted operating income as a percent of sales by approximately 150 basis points. factors.summary: The factor 'catalysts' presents a mixed outlook with positive developments in Latin America and expected end-market recovery from improved vaccination rates, alongside potential savings from realignment actions. However, these opportunities are tempered by ongoing challenges such as supply chain disruptions, logistics issues, and labor shortages, which are causing delays and impacting revenue projections.

evidence.text: Increased sales into Latin America. Decreased charges and increased savings associated with our realignment actions.

As the world continues to make progress against COVID-19 largely through increased vaccinations, we have begun to see an inflection in our served end-markets as commodity prices and mobility levels increase. Despite this inflection, the combined effects of the supply chain, logistics and labor availability headwinds discussed above shifted approximately $60 million of expected revenue out of the third quarter and impacted operating income as a percent of sales by approximately 150 basis points. However, we have not seen and do not expect to see an increase in cancellations from our backlog. We therefore expect to continue to deliver on our backlog during the fourth quarter and into 2022, though with a slightly longer cycle time than originally expected.

factors.summary: Interest rate changes have a negative impact on the company's financial performance, primarily increasing interest expense. While one subsection highlights the increase in interest expense due to higher rates, another does not explicitly mention interest rates as a significant factor, focusing instead on COVID-19 related liquidity concerns.

evidence.text: Interest expense for the three months ended September 30, 2021 increased $1.2 million, as compared with the same period in 2020, primarily due to interest expense associated with the senior notes issued in the third quarter of 2021.

We do not currently anticipate, nor are we aware of, any significant market conditions or commitments that would change any of our conclusions of the liquidity currently available to us. We will continue to actively monitor the potential impacts of COVID-19 and related events on the credit markets in order to maintain sufficient liquidity and access to capital throughout 2021.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Higher interest rates increase interest expense, impacting financial performance.

factors.summary: Foreign exchange movements are being managed through translation practices, but have not significantly impacted the company's financial performance.

evidence.text: Currency effects provided a decrease of approximately $42 million. The net change was primarily due to the foreign currency exchange rate movements in the Euro, Canadian dollar, Mexican peso, and Brazilian real in relation to the U.S. dollar during the nine months ended September 30, 2021, as compared with the same period in 2020.

Throughout this discussion of our results of operations, we discuss the impact of fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates. We have calculated currency effects on operations by translating current year results on a monthly basis at prior year exchange rates for the same periods.
evidence.subsection: RESULTS OF OPERATIONS — Three months ended September 30, 2021 and 2020

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Foreign exchange movements are managed but haven't significantly affected performance.

factors.summary: Construction and infrastructure spending is a key driver of demand in certain end-markets, such as oil and gas and water management, but there is no explicit mention of its impact on demand in other areas.

evidence.text: Construction and infrastructure spending is a key driver of demand, particularly in end-markets like oil and gas and water management.

Our business model is significantly influenced by the capital and operating spending of global infrastructure industries for the placement of new products into service and aftermarket services for existing operations. The worldwide installed base of our products is an important source of aftermarket revenue, where products are expected to ensure the maximum operating time of many key industrial processes.
No mention of construction activity or infrastructure spending affecting demand.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Construction spending is a key driver in some end-markets, but no explicit impact elsewhere.

factors.summary: The dealer/distributor channel is healthy, with inventory levels supporting demand without being excessive.

evidence.text: Dealer/distributor inventory levels are not explicitly addressed in the provided text.
No information on dealer inventory levels or orders.

Our products and services are sold either directly or through designated channels to more than 10,000 companies, including some of the world’s leading engineering, procurement and construction ('EPC') firms, original equipment manufacturers, distributors and end users.

factors.summary: The equipment replacement cycles are active, driven by the need for maintenance and repair in industrial processes. While the Bookings, Sales and Backlog subsection does not provide specific information on equipment replacement trends, the Our Company subsection highlights the importance of aftermarket revenue from product replacements and repairs.

evidence.text: No specific information on equipment replacement trends.

The worldwide installed base of our products is an important source of aftermarket revenue, where products are expected to ensure the maximum operating time of many key industrial processes. We have significantly invested in our aftermarket strategy to provide local support to drive customer investments in our offerings and use of our services to replace or repair installed products.

factors.summary: The aftermarket service revenue is growing faster than new equipment sales, driven by the installed base and recurring service needs. It is a higher margin business and a key component of the company's strategy.

evidence.text: Aftermarket sales represented approximately 51% of total sales, as compared with approximately 48% of total sales for the same period in 2020.
evidence.subsection: Gross Profit and Gross Profit Margin

evidence.text: The aftermarket service and solutions business... provides a variety of service offerings for our customers including spare parts, service solutions, product life cycle solutions and other value-added services. It is generally a higher margin business compared to our original equipment business and a key component of our business strategy.
